parameters.name: Resting Heart Rate
parameters.simplyPut: How fast your heart beats when you're sitting still — lower is usually better.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Lower resting HR generally indicates better cardiovascular fitness. Newborns have higher rates (100–160 bpm).

nutritionLinks.effect: May reduce resting heart rate by 1–3 bpm
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Improves cardiac autonomic function and vagal tone by modulating ion channels in cardiac pacemaker cells

nutritionLinks.effect: Helps regulate heart rhythm and may lower RHR
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Acts as a natural calcium channel blocker, stabilizing cardiac electrical conduction

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces RHR by 5–25 bpm with regular training over weeks to months
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases stroke volume and parasympathetic tone, reducing sympathetic drive
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150 min/week moderate or 75 min/week vigorous aerobic activity

interconnections.relationship: Elevated RHR is associated with higher systolic blood pressure through shared sympathetic activation
Lower RHR typically correlates with higher HRV, both reflecting greater parasympathetic tone
Lower RHR generally indicates higher VO2 max and better aerobic fitness
Fever increases heart rate by approximately 10 bpm per 1°F (0.6°C) increase in temperature
Low oxygen saturation triggers compensatory increase in heart rate to maintain oxygen delivery
Thyroid hormone excess (low TSH) causes tachycardia; hypothyroidism (high TSH) can cause bradycardia

citations.text: Mozaffarian D, Geelen A, Brouwer IA, et al. Effect of fish oil on heart rate in humans: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. Circulation. 2005;112(13):1945-1952.
DiNicolantonio JJ, Liu J, O'Keefe JH. Magnesium for the prevention and treatment of cardiovascular disease. Open Heart. 2018;5(2):e000775.
American College of Sports Medicine. ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.

parameters.name: Systolic Blood Pressure
parameters.simplyPut: The pressure in your arteries when your heart pumps — the top number in a blood pressure reading.
parameters.normalRange: <120 mmHg (normal), 120–129 mmHg (elevated), 130–139 mmHg (Stage 1 HTN), ≥140 mmHg (Stage 2 HTN)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Blood pressure varies throughout the day. Morning readings tend to be higher. Average of multiple readings is more reliable.

nutritionLinks.effect: Reducing sodium intake by 1000 mg/day lowers SBP by 3–5 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Reduces extracellular fluid volume and arterial stiffness

nutritionLinks.effect: Adequate potassium intake (3500–5000 mg/day) can lower SBP by 4–5 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Promotes renal sodium excretion and vascular relaxation

nutritionLinks.effect: Reduces SBP by 8–14 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Combined effect of high potassium, calcium, magnesium, fiber, and low saturated fat and sodium

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces SBP by 5–8 mmHg in hypertensive individuals
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Reduces peripheral vascular resistance, improves endothelial function, and lowers sympathetic tone
exerciseLinks.recommendation: At least 150 min/week of moderate-intensity aerobic activity (brisk walking, cycling, swimming)

exerciseLinks.effect: May reduce SBP by 5–7 mmHg
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Improves vascular function and autonomic regulation after sustained contractions
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 4 × 2 min handgrip exercises at 30% maximal voluntary contraction, 3 days/week

interconnections.relationship: Systolic and diastolic pressures are measured together; isolated systolic hypertension is common in elderly
Elevated heart rate increases cardiac output contributing to higher systolic pressure
Uncontrolled SBP accelerates kidney damage reflected in declining eGFR
High sodium intake increases blood volume and systolic pressure through fluid retention
Higher BMI is strongly associated with higher systolic blood pressure through multiple mechanisms
Diabetes (elevated HbA1c) accelerates atherosclerosis and contributes to systolic hypertension

citations.text: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Hypertension. 2018;71(6):e13-e115.

parameters.name: Diastolic Blood Pressure
parameters.simplyPut: The pressure in your arteries between heartbeats — the bottom number in a blood pressure reading.
parameters.normalRange: <80 mmHg (normal), 80–89 mmHg (Stage 1 HTN), ≥90 mmHg (Stage 2 HTN)
parameters.normalRangeNote: DBP tends to rise until age 55, then declines as arteries stiffen, leading to widened pulse pressure in elderly.

nutritionLinks.effect: Supplementation may reduce DBP by 2–3 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Relaxes vascular smooth muscle and improves endothelial function

nutritionLinks.effect: Can acutely lower DBP by 2–5 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Converted to nitric oxide in the body, promoting vasodilation

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces DBP by 4–6 mmHg
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Reduces peripheral vascular resistance through improved endothelial function and reduced sympathetic tone
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 30–60 minutes of moderate intensity exercise most days of the week

interconnections.relationship: Together define blood pressure status; pulse pressure (SBP minus DBP) is an independent cardiovascular risk marker
Sustained diastolic hypertension contributes to nephrosclerosis and declining kidney function
Heart rate affects diastolic filling time; tachycardia can raise diastolic pressure
Sodium excess raises both systolic and diastolic blood pressure through volume expansion

citations.text: Zhang X, Li Y, Del Gobbo LC, et al. Effects of Magnesium Supplementation on Blood Pressure: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Trials. Hypertension. 2016;68(2):324-333.
Siervo M, Lara J, Ogbonmwan I, Mathers JC. Inorganic nitrate and beetroot juice supplementation reduces blood pressure in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Nutr. 2013;143(6):818-826.
Cornelissen VA, Smart NA. Exercise training for blood pressure: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Heart Assoc. 2013;2(1):e004473.
World Health Organization. Guideline for the pharmacological treatment of hypertension in adults. WHO; 2021.

parameters.name: Blood Pressure Trend
parameters.simplyPut: Whether your blood pressure is going up, down, or staying steady over time.
parameters.normalRange: Average home BP <135/85 mmHg; average 24-hour ABPM <130/80 mmHg
parameters.normalRangeNote: Consistent readings above threshold on multiple occasions confirm hypertension diagnosis. A single elevated reading is insufficient.

nutritionLinks.effect: Consistent low-sodium diet improves BP trends over weeks
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Sustained reduction in extracellular volume and vascular reactivity

nutritionLinks.effect: Reducing alcohol improves BP trends; each drink eliminated reduces SBP ~1 mmHg
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Alcohol raises BP through sympathetic activation, RAAS stimulation, and endothelial dysfunction

exerciseLinks.effect: Regular exercise shows BP trend improvement within 4–12 weeks
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Sustained reduction in peripheral resistance, improved baroreflex sensitivity, and reduced arterial stiffness
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Maintain consistent exercise schedule for meaningful trend improvements; avoid sporadic high-intensity sessions

interconnections.relationship: Trend analysis smooths out individual systolic readings to reveal true BP status
Diverging SBP and DBP trends may indicate arterial stiffening with aging
Weight trends strongly correlate with BP trends; 1 kg weight loss ≈ 1 mmHg SBP reduction
Dietary sodium changes directly affect blood pressure trends over days to weeks

citations.text: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(19):e127-e248.
Pescatello LS, et al. Physical Activity to Prevent and Treat Hypertension: A Systematic Review. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2019;51(6):1314-1323.

parameters.name: Blood Oxygen Saturation
parameters.simplyPut: How much oxygen your blood is carrying — healthy lungs keep this above 95%.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Values below 94% at sea level are considered abnormal. Readings can be affected by nail polish, cold fingers, poor perfusion, and skin pigmentation.

nutritionLinks.effect: Adequate iron ensures sufficient hemoglobin for oxygen transport
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Iron is the central atom in heme groups that bind oxygen in hemoglobin molecules

nutritionLinks.effect: Enhances iron absorption supporting hemoglobin synthesis
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Reduces ferric iron to ferrous form in the gut, increasing bioavailability; also acts as antioxidant in lung tissue

exerciseLinks.effect: Improves oxygen extraction efficiency at tissue level and lung capacity
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases capillary density in muscles, improves ventilation-perfusion matching, and enhances mitochondrial function
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Regular moderate aerobic activity; altitude training can improve oxygen-carrying capacity

interconnections.relationship: Low hemoglobin reduces total oxygen-carrying capacity even if SpO2 percentage is normal
Heart rate increases compensatorily when SpO2 drops to maintain oxygen delivery to tissues
Reduced lung function (FEV1) impairs gas exchange and can lower SpO2
Low SpO2 triggers increased respiratory rate as a compensatory mechanism

citations.text: World Health Organization. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia and assessment of severity. WHO/NMH/NHD/MNM/11.1. Geneva: WHO; 2011.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 41: Transport of Oxygen and Carbon Dioxide.

parameters.name: Body Temperature
parameters.simplyPut: Your body's internal heat level — a fever usually means your immune system is fighting something.
parameters.normalRange: 97.8–99.1°F (36.6–37.3°C) orally; varies with time of day and individual
parameters.normalRangeNote: The classic 98.6°F (37°C) is an average. Individual baselines vary. Body temperature has declined ~0.03°C per decade since the 1800s.

nutritionLinks.effect: Sufficient calories maintain thermogenesis
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Diet-induced thermogenesis accounts for 10% of energy expenditure; severe caloric restriction lowers body temperature

nutritionLinks.effect: Temporarily increases body temperature and metabolic rate
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Activates TRPV1 receptors triggering sympathetic thermogenesis

exerciseLinks.effect: Increases core temperature by 1–3°C during exercise
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Muscular contraction generates heat; thermoregulation redistributes blood flow to skin for cooling
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Be aware of hyperthermia risk during intense exercise in hot environments; acclimatize gradually

interconnections.relationship: Heart rate increases ~10 bpm per 1°C rise in body temperature during fever
Fever typically accompanies elevated white blood cell count during infection
Fever and CRP elevation often occur together during acute inflammation and infection
Fever increases metabolic rate and CO2 production, raising respiratory rate

citations.text: Protsiv M, Ley C, Lankester J, Hastie T, Parsonnet J. Decreasing human body temperature in the United States since the Industrial Revolution. eLife. 2020;9:e49555.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 74: Body Temperature Regulation and Fever.
World Health Organization. Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI) chart booklet. WHO; 2014.

parameters.name: Hemoglobin
parameters.simplyPut: The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen — low levels mean anemia, which causes fatigue.
parameters.normalRange: Men: 13.5–17.5 g/dL; Women: 12.0–16.0 g/dL; Pregnant women: 11.0–14.0 g/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: Values vary with altitude (higher at altitude), hydration status, and pregnancy. WHO defines anemia as Hgb <13 g/dL in men and <12 g/dL in women.

nutritionLinks.effect: Iron supplementation corrects iron deficiency anemia, raising Hgb by 1–2 g/dL over 4–8 weeks
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Iron is incorporated into heme groups during erythropoiesis in bone marrow

nutritionLinks.effect: Correct megaloblastic anemia with macrocytic red blood cells
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Essential for DNA synthesis during red blood cell maturation; deficiency causes ineffective erythropoiesis

exerciseLinks.effect: Initially may decrease Hgb concentration (sports anemia) due to plasma volume expansion, but total Hgb mass increases
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise stimulates erythropoiesis; plasma expansion dilutes concentration but total oxygen-carrying capacity improves
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Ensure adequate iron intake, especially in female athletes and endurance runners (foot-strike hemolysis and sweat losses)

interconnections.relationship: SpO2 may be normal even with low Hgb, but total oxygen delivery is reduced (anemic hypoxia)
Anemia causes compensatory tachycardia to maintain oxygen delivery
Ferritin reflects iron stores; low ferritin precedes hemoglobin decline in iron deficiency
B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia with low hemoglobin and high MCV
Kidney disease reduces erythropoietin production, causing anemia (low hemoglobin)

citations.text: World Health Organization. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia and assessment of severity. WHO/NMH/NHD/MNM/11.1. Geneva: WHO; 2011.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 59: Anemia and Polycythemia.

parameters.name: White Blood Cell Count
parameters.simplyPut: The number of infection-fighting white blood cells — high can mean infection, low can mean immune weakness.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Pregnancy, stress, exercise, and smoking can elevate WBC. Some ethnic groups (e.g., people of African descent) may have lower baseline neutrophil counts (benign ethnic neutropenia).

nutritionLinks.effect: Supports immune cell function and white blood cell production
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Enhances neutrophil chemotaxis and phagocytosis; accumulates in WBCs at concentrations 10–100x higher than plasma

nutritionLinks.effect: Essential for immune cell development and function
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Zinc deficiency impairs T-cell maturation, NK cell activity, and neutrophil function; severe deficiency causes lymphopenia

exerciseLinks.effect: Acutely increases WBC count (exercise leukocytosis); chronic moderate exercise supports healthy immune function
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise mobilizes leukocytes through increased cardiac output and catecholamine release. Chronic training improves immune surveillance.
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150 min/week moderate exercise supports immune function; excessive training may suppress immunity

interconnections.relationship: Fever and elevated WBC often occur together in infection, forming the systemic inflammatory response
WBC count and CRP both rise during inflammation and infection, providing complementary information
Pancytopenia (low WBC, RBC, and platelets) suggests bone marrow failure
Neutrophils are the largest component of WBC; WBC changes usually reflect neutrophil changes
Lymphocytes are the second largest WBC component; viral infections often cause lymphocytosis or lymphopenia

citations.text: Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 60: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes.
Nieman DC, Wentz LM. The compelling link between physical activity and the body's defense system. J Sport Health Sci. 2019;8(3):201-217.
World Health Organization. WHO Laboratory Manual for the Examination and Processing of Human Semen, 6th ed. WHO; 2021.

parameters.name: Platelet Count
parameters.simplyPut: Tiny blood cells that help your blood clot — too few means bleeding risk, too many means clotting risk.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Mild fluctuations are normal. Pregnancy may cause mild thrombocytopenia (gestational). Some individuals have familial low normal counts.

nutritionLinks.effect: Deficiency can cause thrombocytopenia as part of megaloblastic anemia
nutritionLinks.mechanism: B12 and folate are required for DNA synthesis in megakaryocytes (platelet precursors)

nutritionLinks.effect: Iron deficiency can cause reactive thrombocytosis (elevated platelets)
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Thrombopoietin and erythropoietin share structural homology; iron deficiency stimulates both pathways

exerciseLinks.effect: Transiently increases platelet count and activation during exercise
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Platelet mobilization from splenic pool and increased shear stress. In chronic training, baseline platelet function normalizes.
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Regular moderate exercise; patients with severe thrombocytopenia (<50 ×10³/μL) should avoid contact sports and heavy straining

interconnections.relationship: Concurrent low Hgb and platelets (bicytopenia/pancytopenia) suggests bone marrow pathology
Pancytopenia (all three lineages low) raises concern for aplastic anemia, MDS, or marrow infiltration
Platelets and coagulation factors work together for hemostasis; both must be adequate for normal clotting
Platelet consumption with elevated D-dimer suggests disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)

citations.text: Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 111: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 37: Hemostasis and Blood Coagulation.

parameters.name: Fasting Blood Glucose (Self-Monitored)
parameters.simplyPut: Your blood sugar level after not eating overnight — high values may signal diabetes or prediabetes.
parameters.normalRange: Normal: 70–99 mg/dL; Prediabetes: 100–125 mg/dL; Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL (confirmed on two occasions)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Self-monitored values may differ slightly from lab venous glucose. CGMs measure interstitial fluid glucose with 5–15 minute lag behind blood glucose.

nutritionLinks.effect: High-fiber diet reduces fasting glucose by improving insulin sensitivity
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Soluble fiber slows carbohydrate absorption, reduces postprandial glucose spikes, and improves gut microbiome composition

nutritionLinks.effect: Replacing high-GI with low-GI foods improves fasting glucose and HbA1c
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Low-GI foods produce gradual glucose absorption, reducing insulin demand and improving insulin sensitivity over time

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces fasting glucose by 15–25 mg/dL in type 2 diabetes
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise increases glucose uptake through GLUT4 translocation independent of insulin; improves insulin sensitivity for 24–72 hours post-exercise
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150 min/week aerobic + 2–3 resistance sessions/week per ADA guidelines

interconnections.relationship: Fasting glucose contributes to HbA1c, which reflects average glucose over 2–3 months
Insulin resistance raises both fasting glucose and triglycerides through hepatic lipogenesis
Weight loss of 5–10% significantly improves fasting glucose in overweight/obese individuals
Elevated fasting glucose with high insulin suggests insulin resistance; with low insulin suggests beta-cell failure
Higher BMI strongly correlates with insulin resistance and elevated fasting glucose

citations.text: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.
Colberg SR, Sigal RJ, Yardley JE, et al. Physical Activity/Exercise and Diabetes: A Position Statement of the ADA. Diabetes Care. 2016;39(11):2065-2079.
World Health Organization. Classification of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2019.

parameters.name: Hemoglobin A1c
parameters.simplyPut: Shows your average blood sugar over the past 3 months — like a report card for diabetes risk.
parameters.normalRange: Normal: <5.7%; Prediabetes: 5.7–6.4%; Diabetes: ≥6.5%
parameters.normalRangeNote: May be inaccurate in hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell, thalassemia), hemolytic anemia, recent transfusion, iron deficiency, and pregnancy. Fructosamine is an alternative in these situations.

nutritionLinks.effect: Reduces HbA1c by 0.3–0.5% in type 2 diabetes
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Rich in fiber, healthy fats, and polyphenols that improve insulin sensitivity and reduce postprandial glycemia

nutritionLinks.effect: May modestly reduce HbA1c in chromium-deficient individuals
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Enhances insulin receptor signaling through chromodulin, improving glucose uptake

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces HbA1c by 0.5–0.7% in type 2 diabetes
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Aerobic exercise improves insulin sensitivity; resistance training increases glucose-storing muscle mass; combined effect is additive
exerciseLinks.recommendation: ADA recommends 150+ min/week moderate aerobic activity plus 2–3 resistance training sessions

interconnections.relationship: HbA1c integrates fasting and postprandial glucose contributions over 2–3 months
Poor glycemic control (high HbA1c) accelerates diabetic kidney disease progression
Hyperglycemia and insulin resistance drive hypertriglyceridemia
HbA1c requires stable hemoglobin; hemolysis, transfusions, and hemoglobin variants affect accuracy

citations.text: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.

Umpierre D, Ribeiro PA, Kramer CK, et al. Physical activity advice only or structured exercise training and association with HbA1c levels in type 2 diabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA. 2011;305(17):1790-1799.
World Health Organization. Use of glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) in the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2011.

parameters.name: Fasting Plasma Glucose (Lab)
parameters.simplyPut: A lab-precise measurement of your blood sugar after fasting — the gold standard for diabetes screening.
parameters.normalRange: Normal: 70–99 mg/dL; Impaired Fasting Glucose (prediabetes): 100–125 mg/dL; Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: Must be confirmed on a repeat test unless unequivocal hyperglycemia with symptoms. Sample processing delay can cause falsely low results.

nutritionLinks.effect: 5–7% weight loss reduces diabetes risk by 58% in prediabetes
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Reduces visceral adiposity, hepatic glucose output, and insulin resistance

nutritionLinks.effect: May modestly reduce fasting glucose by 3–5%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Improves insulin receptor sensitivity and enhances glucose transporter activity

exerciseLinks.effect: 150 min/week moderate exercise reduces diabetes incidence by 58%
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Improves whole-body insulin sensitivity, reduces hepatic glucose output, and promotes weight loss
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Brisk walking 30 minutes, 5 days per week, combined with 5–7% weight loss

interconnections.relationship: Both FPG and HbA1c are diagnostic for diabetes; concordance strengthens diagnosis
Obesity (high BMI) is the strongest modifiable risk factor for elevated fasting glucose and type 2 diabetes
Fasting glucose is one of the two inputs for HOMA-IR calculation (with fasting insulin)

citations.text: Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med. 2002;346(6):393-403.
American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.
World Health Organization. Classification of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2019.

parameters.name: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone
parameters.simplyPut: Controls your thyroid gland — too high or too low affects your energy, weight, and mood.
parameters.normalRange: 0.4–4.0 mIU/L (some experts advocate upper limit of 2.5 mIU/L)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Pregnancy: first trimester 0.1–2.5 mIU/L. Elderly may have slightly higher TSH. Biotin supplements can interfere with immunoassays.

nutritionLinks.effect: Adequate iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Iodine is incorporated into thyroid hormones T3 and T4. Both deficiency and excess can cause thyroid dysfunction.

nutritionLinks.effect: Supports thyroid hormone metabolism and may reduce thyroid antibody levels in Hashimoto's
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Selenoproteins (deiodinases, glutathione peroxidase) are essential for T4 to T3 conversion and protection from oxidative damage

exerciseLinks.effect: Exercise supports healthy thyroid function; hypothyroidism symptoms improve with activity
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise increases tissue sensitivity to thyroid hormones, improves T4 to T3 conversion, and counteracts metabolic slowing of hypothyroidism
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Regular exercise as adjunct to thyroid hormone replacement. May need dose adjustment with significant weight or fitness changes.

interconnections.relationship: Hypothyroidism causes bradycardia; hyperthyroidism causes tachycardia. Thyroid status affects heart rate.
Hypothyroidism raises cholesterol by reducing LDL receptor expression; always check TSH in new hyperlipidemia
Hypothyroidism causes modest weight gain (5–10 lbs from fluid retention); hyperthyroidism causes weight loss despite increased appetite
TSH and free T4 have an inverse log-linear relationship; small T4 changes cause large TSH changes
TSH stimulates T3 production; in subclinical hypothyroidism, T3 may be maintained despite high TSH

citations.text: Garber JR, Cobin RH, Gharib H, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for Hypothyroidism in Adults. Endocr Pract. 2012;18(6):988-1028.
Jonklaas J, Bianco AC, Bauer AJ, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):1670-1751.
World Health Organization. Assessment of iodine deficiency disorders and monitoring their elimination: a guide for programme managers, 3rd ed. WHO; 2007.

parameters.name: TSH Monitoring (On Treatment)
parameters.simplyPut: Regular checks of your thyroid hormone level while on thyroid medication — ensures proper dosing.
parameters.normalRange: Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients on levothyroxine; 0.1–0.5 for thyroid cancer suppression therapy
parameters.normalRangeNote: Allow 6–8 weeks after dose changes before rechecking TSH. Pregnancy requires more frequent monitoring with trimester-specific targets.

nutritionLinks.effect: Calcium, iron, soy, and coffee reduce levothyroxine absorption
nutritionLinks.mechanism: These substances bind levothyroxine in the gut or alter gastric pH, reducing bioavailability by 20–50%
foods: Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, 30–60 min before food

nutritionLinks.effect: High-fiber diets may reduce levothyroxine absorption
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Fiber binds medication in the gut; effect is modest but relevant at high intakes
foods: Do not avoid fiber — maintain consistent intake and time medication separately

exerciseLinks.effect: May alter thyroid hormone requirements as metabolism and body composition change
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Weight loss and improved fitness may reduce levothyroxine dose requirements
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Recheck TSH after significant weight or activity level changes

interconnections.relationship: Monitoring TSH is the serial version of baseline TSH measurement
New palpitations or tachycardia in a patient on levothyroxine may indicate overtreatment (low TSH)
Both TSH and free T4 should be monitored to ensure appropriate thyroid hormone replacement

citations.text: Jonklaas J, Bianco AC, Bauer AJ, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):1670-1751.
Garber JR, Cobin RH, Gharib H, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for Hypothyroidism in Adults. Endocr Pract. 2012;18(6):988-1028.

parameters.name: Total Cholesterol
parameters.simplyPut: The total amount of cholesterol in your blood — a starting point, but the breakdown matters more.
parameters.normalRange: Desirable: <200 mg/dL; Borderline high: 200–239 mg/dL; High: ≥240 mg/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: Total cholesterol alone is insufficient for risk assessment — LDL, HDL, and triglyceride breakdown is needed.

nutritionLinks.effect: 5–10 g/day reduces TC by 3–5%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Binds bile acids in gut, forcing liver to use cholesterol for new bile acid synthesis

nutritionLinks.effect: 2 g/day reduces LDL by 8–10%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Compete with cholesterol for intestinal absorption, reducing cholesterol entry into the body

exerciseLinks.effect: Raises HDL by 3–6% and may modestly lower LDL and triglycerides
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases lipoprotein lipase activity, enhances reverse cholesterol transport, and improves lipid particle size
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150 min/week moderate or 75 min/week vigorous aerobic activity

interconnections.relationship: LDL comprises 60–70% of total cholesterol and is the primary atherogenic fraction
HDL is cardioprotective; TC/HDL ratio is a useful risk indicator (optimal <4)
VLDL (carrying triglycerides) contributes to total cholesterol; elevated triglycerides raise TC
Hypothyroidism raises total cholesterol; treating thyroid disease often normalizes lipids

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

parameters.name: LDL Cholesterol
parameters.simplyPut: Often called 'bad' cholesterol — high levels build up in artery walls and cause heart disease.
parameters.normalRange: Optimal: <100 mg/dL; Near optimal: 100–129; Borderline high: 130–159; High: 160–189; Very high: ≥190 mg/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: For very high-risk patients (established CVD): target <70 mg/dL. For extremely high risk: <55 mg/dL per ESC guidelines.

nutritionLinks.effect: Replacing 5% of calories from saturated fat with unsaturated fat reduces LDL by 8–10 mg/dL
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Saturated fat upregulates hepatic LDL receptor degradation via PCSK9, reducing LDL clearance

nutritionLinks.effect: Combines plant sterols, soy protein, viscous fiber, and almonds to reduce LDL by 25–30%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Multiple complementary mechanisms targeting cholesterol absorption, synthesis, and excretion

exerciseLinks.effect: Modest LDL reduction of 3–5% with regular exercise; greater when combined with weight loss
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases LDL receptor expression, reduces LDL particle number, and shifts particles from small dense to large buoyant
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Regular aerobic exercise plus dietary changes for maximal LDL reduction

interconnections.relationship: LDL is the major contributor to total cholesterol
Cumulative LDL exposure drives CAC score progression
Diabetes increases small dense LDL particles that are more atherogenic
Each LDL particle carries one apoB; apoB may be more accurate than LDL-C for risk assessment
Statins lower both LDL and CRP; combined LDL and CRP reduction maximizes cardiovascular benefit

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
Cholesterol Treatment Trialists' Collaboration. Efficacy and safety of statin therapy in older people. Lancet. 2019;393(10170):407-415.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

parameters.name: HDL Cholesterol
parameters.simplyPut: Often called 'good' cholesterol — it removes excess cholesterol from arteries and brings it back to the liver.
parameters.normalRange: Men: >40 mg/dL; Women: >50 mg/dL; Optimal: >60 mg/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: HDL <40 mg/dL in men or <50 in women is a cardiovascular risk factor and a component of metabolic syndrome.

nutritionLinks.effect: Replacing carbohydrates with MUFA increases HDL
nutritionLinks.mechanism: MUFA increases apoA-I synthesis, the main structural protein of HDL

nutritionLinks.effect: 1 drink/day increases HDL by 3–4 mg/dL
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Increases apoA-I production and ABCA1-mediated cholesterol efflux
foods: If already drinking: limit to 1/day women, 2/day men. Not recommended to start for HDL benefit.

exerciseLinks.effect: Most effective lifestyle intervention for raising HDL — increases by 2–8 mg/dL
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases lipoprotein lipase activity, promotes HDL maturation, and enhances reverse cholesterol transport
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Volume of exercise matters more than intensity for HDL; aim for 150+ min/week

interconnections.relationship: HDL and triglycerides are inversely related through CETP-mediated lipid exchange; high TG = low HDL
TC/HDL ratio is a useful risk metric; optimal <4
Insulin resistance is the most common cause of low HDL
Central obesity is strongly associated with low HDL and high triglycerides (atherogenic dyslipidemia)

parameters.name: Triglycerides
parameters.simplyPut: Fats in your blood from recent meals and stored energy — high levels raise heart disease and pancreatitis risk.
parameters.normalRange: Normal: <150 mg/dL; Borderline: 150–199; High: 200–499; Very high: ≥500 mg/dL
parameters.normalRangeNote: Fasting is important for accurate TG assessment. Alcohol and high-carbohydrate meals significantly raise levels.

nutritionLinks.effect: Most effective dietary intervention — reduces TG by 20–50%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Excess carbohydrates are converted to triglycerides via de novo lipogenesis in the liver

nutritionLinks.effect: 4 g/day of EPA/DHA reduces TG by 25–45%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Reduces hepatic VLDL production and increases TG clearance through lipoprotein lipase activation
foods: Prescription icosapent ethyl for very high TG

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces TG by 10–30%, especially after recent exercise
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases lipoprotein lipase activity for 24–48 hours post-exercise, clearing TG-rich particles from blood
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Regular aerobic exercise; effect is dose-dependent and transient, so consistency matters

interconnections.relationship: Triglycerides and HDL are inversely related; treating hypertriglyceridemia often raises HDL
Insulin resistance drives both hyperglycemia and hypertriglyceridemia through hepatic overproduction
Elevated TG with elevated ALT suggests non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
Miller M, Stone NJ, Ballantyne C, et al. Triglycerides and Cardiovascular Disease: A Scientific Statement From the AHA. Circulation. 2011;123(20):2292-2333.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

parameters.name: Lipid Panel Monitoring (On Treatment)
parameters.simplyPut: Regular cholesterol checks while on medication — ensures your treatment is working.
parameters.normalRange: On high-intensity statin: expect ≥50% LDL reduction. On moderate-intensity: 30–49% reduction. Specific targets depend on risk category.
parameters.normalRangeNote: If LDL reduction <30% on statin, assess adherence and consider intensifying therapy.

nutritionLinks.effect: Diet adds 15–25% LDL reduction on top of statin therapy
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Reducing dietary saturated fat and adding plant sterols/fiber complements statin mechanism

nutritionLinks.effect: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4, increasing statin levels and toxicity risk
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Furanocoumarin compounds irreversibly inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 enzyme
foods: Avoid grapefruit with simvastatin and lovastatin; less concern with rosuvastatin and pravastatin

exerciseLinks.effect: Complements pharmacological lipid management
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise independently improves lipid profile and cardiovascular fitness beyond what medications achieve
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Exercise is recommended alongside statin therapy; myalgia should be assessed but often improves with exercise

interconnections.relationship: LDL-C is the primary treatment target being monitored
ALT should be checked at baseline and if hepatotoxicity symptoms develop on statins
Statins may slightly increase HbA1c (0.1%); monitor in diabetic and prediabetic patients

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.

parameters.name: Alanine Aminotransferase
parameters.simplyPut: A liver enzyme — high levels usually mean your liver cells are being damaged or inflamed.
parameters.normalRange: Men: 7–56 U/L; Women: 7–45 U/L (updated guidelines suggest upper normal: 33 U/L men, 25 U/L women)
parameters.normalRangeNote: ALT is more liver-specific than AST. Mild elevations (<3× upper normal) are very common and often due to NAFLD or medications.

nutritionLinks.effect: 3+ cups/day reduces ALT and liver disease risk by 40%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Cafestol and kahweol have anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effects. Caffeine reduces hepatic fat accumulation.
foods: Both caffeinated and decaf show benefit

nutritionLinks.effect: Major dietary contributors to elevated ALT
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Fructose drives de novo lipogenesis in the liver causing steatosis; alcohol is directly hepatotoxic through acetaldehyde
foods: Limit alcohol to <2 drinks/day men, <1 women

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces ALT by 10–20% through reduction of hepatic fat
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Exercise reduces intrahepatic triglyceride content through enhanced fatty acid oxidation and reduced de novo lipogenesis
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150+ min/week moderate exercise reduces liver fat even without weight loss

interconnections.relationship: Elevated triglycerides with elevated ALT strongly suggests NAFLD
Obesity is the strongest risk factor for NAFLD and elevated ALT
Diabetes and insulin resistance drive NAFLD progression
ALT is more liver-specific than AST; AST/ALT ratio >2 suggests alcoholic liver disease
ALT and GGT together are sensitive markers of fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MAFLD)

citations.text: Chalasani N, Younossi Z, Lavine JE, et al. The Diagnosis and Management of Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease: Practice Guidance From the AASLD. Hepatology. 2018;67(1):328-357.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 337: Evaluation of Liver Function.

parameters.name: Creatinine / eGFR
parameters.simplyPut: A waste product your kidneys filter out — high levels may mean your kidneys need attention.
parameters.normalRange: Creatinine: Men 0.7–1.3 mg/dL, Women 0.6–1.1 mg/dL; eGFR: >90 mL/min/1.73m² (normal), 60–89 (mildly decreased), 30–59 (moderately decreased), 15–29 (severely decreased), <15 (kidney failure)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Muscular individuals may have higher creatinine with normal kidney function. Cystatin C-based eGFR is more accurate in certain populations.

nutritionLinks.effect: Maintains renal perfusion and GFR
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Dehydration reduces renal blood flow, acutely lowering GFR. Chronic dehydration may contribute to CKD progression.

nutritionLinks.effect: 0.6–0.8 g/kg/day protein may slow CKD progression
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Lower protein intake reduces hyperfiltration, intraglomerular pressure, and uremic toxin generation
foods: Plant-based proteins may be preferable in CKD

exerciseLinks.effect: May slow CKD progression and improve cardiovascular outcomes in CKD
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Reduces blood pressure, inflammation, and insulin resistance — all drivers of CKD progression
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Moderate exercise is safe and beneficial in CKD; avoid extreme exertion that may cause rhabdomyolysis

interconnections.relationship: Hypertension is both a cause and consequence of CKD; BP control slows progression
Diabetes is the leading cause of CKD; glycemic control prevents diabetic nephropathy
Proteinuria plus reduced eGFR defines CKD more precisely than either alone
eGFR and UACR together stage chronic kidney disease and predict progression to kidney failure
Declining eGFR impairs potassium excretion, making hyperkalemia a dangerous complication of CKD

citations.text: KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int Suppl. 2013;3(1):1-150.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 305: Chronic Kidney Disease.

parameters.name: Urinalysis
parameters.simplyPut: A simple urine test that checks for infections, kidney disease, diabetes, and other conditions.
parameters.normalRange: pH: 4.5–8.0; Specific gravity: 1.005–1.030; Protein: negative; Glucose: negative; Blood: negative; WBC: 0–5/HPF; RBC: 0–3/HPF; No casts, bacteria, or crystals
parameters.normalRangeNote: First morning specimen is most concentrated and best for detecting proteinuria. Menstruation can contaminate with blood.

nutritionLinks.effect: Dilute urine reduces kidney stone formation
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Higher urine volume reduces crystal supersaturation and stone nucleation
foods: Water (aim for urine output >2L/day for stone prevention)

nutritionLinks.nutrient: Reduce sodium and animal protein for stone prevention
nutritionLinks.effect: Reduces calcium and uric acid stone risk
nutritionLinks.mechanism: High sodium increases urinary calcium excretion; animal protein increases uric acid and reduces citrate

exerciseLinks.effect: Intense exercise can cause transient proteinuria and hematuria
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Renal vasoconstriction during exercise reduces GFR; mechanical trauma from running can cause hematuria
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Exercise-induced proteinuria and hematuria are benign and resolve within 24–48 hours; persistent findings need evaluation

interconnections.relationship: Proteinuria quantification combined with eGFR defines CKD stage and prognosis
Glucosuria on UA may be the first indication of undiagnosed diabetes

citations.text: KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int Suppl. 2013;3(1):1-150.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 51: Urinary Abnormalities.

parameters.name: Sleep Duration
parameters.simplyPut: How many hours you sleep per night — most adults need 7-9 hours for optimal health.
parameters.normalRange: 7–9 hours (ages 18–64); 7–8 hours (ages 65+)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Individual sleep need varies. Consistently <6 hours or >9 hours is associated with adverse outcomes.

nutritionLinks.effect: May improve sleep quality and duration in those with deficiency
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Regulates GABA receptors and melatonin production, promoting nervous system relaxation

nutritionLinks.effect: Precursor to serotonin and melatonin, may promote sleep onset
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Converted to serotonin then melatonin in the pineal gland via rate-limited enzymatic steps

exerciseLinks.effect: Improves sleep quality, reduces sleep onset latency, and increases total sleep time
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases adenosine accumulation, body temperature followed by post-exercise cooling, and reduces anxiety through endorphin release
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 30 min moderate exercise most days, ideally completed 4–6 hours before bedtime

interconnections.relationship: Poor sleep directly worsens mood and stress reactivity; stress hormones like cortisol in turn disrupt sleep architecture
Sleep deprivation elevates resting heart rate through sympathetic activation
Even one night of sleep restriction impairs next-day glucose metabolism
Sleep disruption elevates cortisol; chronic sleep deprivation flattens the normal circadian cortisol rhythm
Short sleep duration (<6h) is associated with weight gain through hormonal changes (ghrelin/leptin) and behavioral factors

parameters.name: Mood & Stress Level
parameters.simplyPut: A self-assessment of your emotional wellbeing and stress level — affects nearly every aspect of health.
parameters.normalRange: PSS-10 score 0–13 (low stress); PHQ-9 score 0–4 (minimal depression); GAD-7 score 0–4 (minimal anxiety)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Scores are subjective and context-dependent. Trends over time are more meaningful than single measurements.

nutritionLinks.effect: Reduce inflammation and may lower anxiety symptoms by 20%
nutritionLinks.mechanism: EPA competes with arachidonic acid, reducing pro-inflammatory eicosanoids that contribute to neuroinflammation

nutritionLinks.effect: Support neurotransmitter synthesis and stress resilience
nutritionLinks.mechanism: B6 is a cofactor for serotonin and GABA synthesis; B12 and folate support methylation and SAMe production

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces depression and anxiety symptoms with effect sizes comparable to pharmacotherapy
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases BDNF, serotonin, endorphins, and endocannabinoids; reduces cortisol and inflammatory cytokines
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150 min/week moderate or 75 min vigorous; even 30 min of brisk walking acutely reduces stress

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces perceived stress by 25–30% and improves HRV
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Activates parasympathetic nervous system, reduces cortisol, enhances GABA activity
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 2–3 sessions per week of 45–60 minutes

interconnections.relationship: High stress disrupts sleep; sleep deprivation amplifies emotional reactivity and stress perception
Perceived stress drives HPA axis activation and cortisol secretion
Chronic stress elevates sympathetic tone, increasing resting heart rate
Low serotonin is associated with depression and anxiety; serotonin-targeting medications improve mood

parameters.name: Vitamin D (25-Hydroxyvitamin D)
parameters.simplyPut: The 'sunshine vitamin' — essential for strong bones, immune defense, and mood; deficiency is very common worldwide.
parameters.normalRange: Sufficient: 30–100 ng/mL (75–250 nmol/L). Insufficient: 20–29 ng/mL. Deficient: <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L).
parameters.normalRangeNote: Endocrine Society recommends ≥30 ng/mL. IOM considers ≥20 ng/mL adequate for bone health. Higher latitude, darker skin, obesity, and age increase deficiency risk.

nutritionLinks.effect: Supplementation raises 25(OH)D levels effectively
nutritionLinks.mechanism: D3 from diet/supplements absorbed in small intestine, hydroxylated in liver to 25(OH)D, then in kidney to active 1,25(OH)2D

exerciseLinks.effect: UVB exposure during outdoor exercise supports vitamin D synthesis
exerciseLinks.mechanism: UVB radiation converts 7-dehydrocholesterol in skin to previtamin D3; 10–30 minutes of midday sun exposure produces 10,000–20,000 IU
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Balance sun exposure for D synthesis with skin cancer prevention. Supplement 1,000–2,000 IU/day if outdoor exposure is limited

interconnections.relationship: Vitamin D is essential for intestinal calcium absorption; deficiency reduces absorption from 30–40% to 10–15%
Vitamin D deficiency accelerates bone loss and increases fracture risk
Low vitamin D triggers compensatory PTH elevation (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Vitamin D deficiency is associated with higher IgE levels and increased allergic sensitization
Vitamin D deficiency triggers secondary hyperparathyroidism as the body tries to maintain calcium levels
Vitamin D modulates immune function; deficiency is associated with higher inflammatory markers including CRP

citations.text: Holick MF, Binkley NC, Bischoff-Ferrari HA, et al. Evaluation, treatment, and prevention of vitamin D deficiency: an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011;96(7):1911-1930.
Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Calcium and Vitamin D. National Academies Press; 2011.
World Health Organization. Nutritional rickets: a review of disease burden, causes, diagnosis, prevention and treatment. WHO; 2019.

parameters.name: Body Mass Index
parameters.simplyPut: Your weight relative to your height — a simple screening tool for underweight, overweight, and obesity.
parameters.normalRange: Underweight: <18.5. Normal: 18.5–24.9. Overweight: 25.0–29.9. Obese I: 30.0–34.9. Obese II: 35.0–39.9. Obese III: ≥40.0 kg/m².
parameters.normalRangeNote: WHO Asian-Pacific guidelines use lower cutoffs: overweight ≥23, obese ≥25. Athletes with high muscle mass may have elevated BMI without excess fat. BMI underestimates body fat in elderly with sarcopenia.

nutritionLinks.effect: Caloric deficit of 500–750 kcal/day produces 0.5–0.75 kg/week weight loss
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Energy balance determines body weight; sustained caloric deficit mobilizes fat stores. Protein (1.2–1.6 g/kg) preserves lean mass during weight loss.

nutritionLinks.effect: Higher fiber intake associated with lower BMI and reduced obesity risk
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Fiber increases satiety, slows gastric emptying, reduces energy density of diet, and promotes beneficial gut microbiome

exerciseLinks.effect: Most effective for BMI reduction and body composition improvement
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Aerobic exercise increases energy expenditure; resistance training preserves lean mass, maintaining metabolic rate during weight loss
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150–300 min/week moderate aerobic activity plus 2+ days resistance training for weight management

interconnections.relationship: Waist circumference adds abdominal obesity assessment that BMI misses; together they better predict cardiometabolic risk
Body fat percentage is more accurate than BMI for assessing adiposity, especially in athletes
Higher BMI increases insulin resistance and fasting glucose
Adipose tissue produces inflammatory cytokines; BMI strongly correlates with CRP
Each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI raises systolic BP by ~5 mmHg; weight loss is first-line treatment for hypertension
Higher BMI strongly predicts Type 2 diabetes; 5-10% weight loss significantly improves HbA1c

parameters.name: Body Fat Percentage
parameters.simplyPut: What fraction of your total body weight is fat — more informative than BMI for fitness assessment.
parameters.normalRange: Men: Essential fat 2–5%, Athletes 6–13%, Fitness 14–17%, Acceptable 18–24%, Obese >25%. Women: Essential fat 10–13%, Athletes 14–20%, Fitness 21–24%, Acceptable 25–31%, Obese >32%.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Body fat increases with age. Ethnic variation exists. Very low body fat (<5% men, <12% women) impairs hormonal function and is unsustainable.

nutritionLinks.effect: Higher protein intake (1.2–1.6 g/kg/day) preserves lean mass during fat loss
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Protein has the highest thermic effect (20–30% of calories), promotes satiety via PYY and GLP-1, and stimulates muscle protein synthesis to preserve lean mass

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces body fat while preserving or increasing lean mass
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Increases resting metabolic rate through muscle hypertrophy; EPOC (excess post-exercise oxygen consumption) increases fat oxidation for 24–48 hours post-exercise
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 2–4 sessions/week with progressive overload; combined with moderate caloric deficit for fat loss

exerciseLinks.effect: Reduces body fat comparably to steady-state cardio in less time
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Elevates EPOC, growth hormone, and catecholamines; promotes visceral fat oxidation
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 2–3 HIIT sessions per week, alternated with lower-intensity training

interconnections.relationship: Body fat percentage provides more accurate adiposity assessment than BMI, especially in muscular or sarcopenic individuals
Waist circumference correlates with visceral fat, the most metabolically active fat depot
Excess body fat, particularly visceral, drives insulin resistance through adipokine and free fatty acid release
Leptin is produced in proportion to fat mass; higher body fat percentage means higher leptin levels
Higher body fat percentage relative to muscle mass lowers BMR per kilogram of body weight

citations.text: Westerterp-Plantenga MS, Lemmens SG, Westerterp KR. Dietary protein — its role in satiety, energetics, weight loss and health. Br J Nutr. 2012;108 Suppl 2:S105-S112.
Westcott WL. Resistance training is medicine: effects of strength training on health. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2012;11(4):209-216.

Wewege M, van den Berg R, Ward RE, Keech A. The effects of high-intensity interval training vs. moderate-intensity continuous training on body composition in overweight and obese adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Obes Rev. 2017;18(6):635-646.

parameters.name: Waist Circumference
parameters.simplyPut: The distance around your belly at the navel — excess belly fat strongly predicts heart disease and diabetes risk.
parameters.normalRange: Men: <94 cm (<37 inches) normal, 94–102 cm increased risk, >102 cm (>40 inches) substantially increased risk. Women: <80 cm (<31.5 inches) normal, 80–88 cm increased risk, >88 cm (>35 inches) substantially increased risk.
parameters.normalRangeNote: Ethnic-specific cutoffs: South Asian, Chinese, Japanese men ≥90 cm and women ≥80 cm indicate increased risk. IDF uses lower thresholds for metabolic syndrome diagnosis.

nutritionLinks.effect: Reduces waist circumference and visceral fat more effectively than low-fat diets
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Olive oil monounsaturated fats, fiber, and polyphenols improve insulin sensitivity and reduce visceral fat deposition

nutritionLinks.effect: Reducing added sugars, especially fructose, decreases visceral fat
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Hepatic fructose metabolism promotes de novo lipogenesis and visceral fat accumulation; fructose does not stimulate insulin or leptin

exerciseLinks.effect: Preferentially reduces visceral fat, even without weight loss
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Visceral fat has higher blood flow and catecholamine receptor density, making it more responsive to exercise-induced lipolysis than subcutaneous fat
exerciseLinks.recommendation: 150–300 min/week moderate or 75–150 min vigorous; higher volumes produce greater visceral fat reduction

interconnections.relationship: Waist circumference adds visceral fat assessment to BMI; together they improve cardiometabolic risk prediction
Waist circumference specifically reflects abdominal/visceral fat distribution
Visceral adiposity drives hepatic VLDL production, elevating triglycerides
Central obesity is the strongest driver of insulin resistance
Waist circumference is the simplest proxy for visceral fat; they correlate strongly
Central adiposity is the strongest clinical predictor of insulin resistance
Large waist circumference predicts low HDL; the combination strongly identifies metabolic syndrome

citations.text: Estruch R, Ros E, Salas-Salvadó J, et al. Primary prevention of cardiovascular disease with a Mediterranean diet supplemented with extra-virgin olive oil or nuts. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(25):e34.

Stanhope KL, Schwarz JM, Keim NL, et al. Consuming fructose-sweetened, not glucose-sweetened, beverages increases visceral adiposity and lipids and decreases insulin sensitivity in overweight/obese humans. J Clin Invest. 2009;119(5):1322-1334.
Vissers D, Hens W, Brouwers J, et al. The effect of exercise on visceral adipose tissue in overweight adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2013;8(2):e56415.
World Health Organization. Waist circumference and waist-hip ratio: report of a WHO expert consultation. WHO; 2008.

parameters.name: Menstrual Cycle Tracking
parameters.simplyPut: Tracking your period's timing, length, and symptoms — irregular cycles may signal hormonal imbalances.
parameters.normalRange: Cycle length: 21–35 days; Flow duration: 3–7 days; Blood loss: 5–80 mL per cycle (>80 mL = heavy menstrual bleeding)
parameters.normalRangeNote: Adolescents may have cycles up to 45 days for first 2 years. Perimenopause (age 40–55) causes increasing irregularity. Absence of periods for >90 days (non-pregnant) warrants evaluation.

nutritionLinks.effect: Women with heavy menstrual bleeding are at high risk of iron deficiency and should optimize iron intake
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Menstrual blood loss is the primary cause of iron deficiency in premenopausal women. Losing >80 mL per cycle depletes iron stores faster than dietary intake can replace them
foods: Pair with vitamin C for enhanced absorption

nutritionLinks.effect: Sufficient energy intake is essential for maintaining regular menstrual cycles
nutritionLinks.mechanism: Caloric restriction below ~30 kcal/kg lean body mass/day suppresses GnRH pulsatility, leading to anovulation and amenorrhea (the female athlete triad / RED-S)
foods: Balanced meals with adequate carbohydrates, fats, and protein
Ensure caloric intake matches energy expenditure, especially in athletes

exerciseLinks.effect: Adjusting exercise intensity across the menstrual cycle may optimize performance and reduce injury risk
exerciseLinks.mechanism: Hormonal fluctuations affect muscle strength, tendon laxity, thermoregulation, and substrate utilization. Estrogen peaks (late follicular) may support strength; the luteal phase raises core temperature and alters fuel use
exerciseLinks.recommendation: Track cycles alongside training. Ensure energy availability (>45 kcal/kg FFM/day). Menstrual loss during intense training is a RED-S warning sign requiring immediate attention

interconnections.relationship: Regular cycles with consistent 12–14 day luteal phases confirm ovulation and adequate progesterone. Irregular cycles often reflect anovulation with absent progesterone
Cycle irregularity with elevated FSH suggests diminished ovarian reserve or premature ovarian insufficiency; with normal/low FSH suggests hypothalamic or PCOS origin
Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism cause menstrual irregularities. TSH is a routine part of the amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea workup
Estradiol peaks trigger the LH surge and ovulation; low estradiol causes amenorrhea

citations.text: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Menstruation in Girls and Adolescents: Using the Menstrual Cycle as a Vital Sign. ACOG Committee Opinion No. 651. Obstet Gynecol. 2015;126(6):e143-e146.
Mountjoy M, Sundgot-Borgen JK, Burke LM, et al. IOC consensus statement on relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S): 2018 update. Br J Sports Med. 2018;52(11):687-697.
